posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I was mortified thinking my streak of recent bad bottles continued. From a super-clean, mature bottle, the cap cut perfectly. As I’m wiping the funk off the top of the cork, the damn thing pushed in. Not a subtle depression, but a whole-scale move, dropping into the bottle. I’m certain it’s dead.

What pours out is gorgeously aromatic.

Actually, the scent was like walking through an old Cuban cigar factory, with sweaty workers diligently plying their trade, wrapping their matured Maduro leaves into a lovely figurado, then gently placing them into cedar boxes. Now mind you, I’m Cuban, but this is all made up, I’ve never walked through a cigar factory. I don’t even know if they call them factories - certainly there is a more quaint term. But this is pretty much how I envision it, and intake this old-school beauty. I swirl the elixir in my bowl, enjoying the wafts emanating from the glass, a range of red to dark and plummy fruits emerge, and autumn forest floor, but the tobacco and pencil shavings predominate. Mid-weight on the palate, tannins have resolved, leaving refreshing acids to give structure to the body of relatively ripe fruits and sweet dark tobacco leaves. Has a moderately grainy presence. This wine is perfectly at apogee.
(94 pts.)
Lucky Man! Sounds like fun.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Neal my friend, you would be all over this wine. MarcF and I shared a 1982 a couple of years ago, and it shined even more. This Chateau way out-performed in these vintages.

posts.name: Marc Frontario
posts.cooked: Def. Marc F. wine…great note

posts.name: Scott Watkins
posts.cooked: Great note and thanks! Which reminds me I have a 1970 in the cellar that I probably waited too long to drink.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Scott Watkins:

Great note and thanks! Which reminds me I have a 1970 in the cellar that I probably waited too long to drink.

That’s not what I’ve heard. I’ve heard the ‘70 is drinking beautifully right now and I’ve been on the lookout to buy one.

posts.name: NED VALOIS
posts.cooked: Maybe; Real Fábrica de Tabacos Partagás, Havana - Cuba ?

posts.name: Carlos Delpin
posts.cooked: Robert, there is a book by Guillermo Cabrera Infante called Holy Smoke. It covers the stories and traditions of the Cuban cigar houses. Great read. Your note made reach for a cigar instead of wine!

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked: Sounds wonderful, right up my alley.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Carlos Delpin:

Robert, there is a book by Guillermo Cabrera Infante called Holy Smoke. It covers the stories and traditions of the Cuban cigar houses. Great read. Your note made reach for a cigar instead of wine!

Awesome:

Kirkus Reviews

HOLY SMOKE | Kirkus Reviews

An engaging classic about a ``burning passion''—cigar smoking—is available here more than a decade after its original UK issue. It is passionate, indeed. This rich and juicy book by Cabrera Infante (Infante's Inferno, 1984; Mea Cuba, 1994; etc.)...

Thanks for the book tip, I’ll grab it.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Sounds good, Robert - I haven’t tried a La Lagune for years - looks like I should.

BTW - thread drift - FYI Neal Martin just published a fabulous article on Vinous about Bel Air Marquis d’Aligre.

posts.name: Diane Kessler
posts.cooked: A 1988 was quite lovely a few months ago.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Popped another last night, saved 1/3 of the bottle, and damn, it’s better on night two!!!

A beautiful, earthy, red-fruited Bordeaux with citrus crisp!

Love it.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: The best bottle of 1961 Cheval Blanc I ever drank was just like this - the cork fell in. And initially, I assumed it was dead. But then the whole room filled with beautiful aromas!

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Neal my friend, you would be all over this wine. MarcF and I shared a 1982 a couple of years ago, and it shined even more. This Chateau way out-performed in these vintages.

Yeah, the 1982 was not a bad $7 wine.

Great story. Bordeaux can be pretty tough to kill. And, it can age quite well, even the ones that are somewhat reasonably priced. The hard thing in Bordeaux is knowing which ones are still made correctly.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Popped another last night, saved 1/3 of the bottle, and damn, it’s better on night two!!!

A beautiful, earthy, red-fruited Bordeaux with citrus crisp!

Love it.

I opened another great bottle of the 1985 two Sundays ago. Then forgot about the last glass in the bottle on the counter until tonight. And damn — it was still delicious. No oxidation. Some sort of magic going on at the estate in the ‘80s.

posts.name: Mark Thompson
posts.cooked:
Popped another last night, saved 1/3 of the bottle, and damn, it’s better on night two!!!

A beautiful, earthy, red-fruited Bordeaux with citrus crisp!

Love it.

I opened another great bottle of the 1985 two Sundays ago. Then forgot about the last glass in the bottle on the counter until tonight. And damn — it was still delicious. No oxidation. Some sort of magic going on at the estate in the ‘80s.

Jayson, this is borderline Snopes worthy. You may need to detail the conditions a little better so we can try to replicate! (First with some random ‘80’s Bordeaux before we try to track down the magical elixir)!!?!

posts.name: Marshall Gelb
posts.cooked:
Neal my friend, you would be all over this wine. MarcF and I shared a 1982 a couple of years ago, and it shined even more. This Chateau way out-performed in these vintages.

Truthfully, La Lagune seems to out perform in almost all vintages. To me, one of the most consistently underrated chateaus in all of Bordeaux.


Cheers!
Marshall
